text: The robotic sleeve gastrectomy begins with measuring six centimeters proximal to the pylorus, marking the area with a suture. The surgeon enters the lesser sac and starts taking down the greater omentum along the greater curvature, proceeding towards the pylorus. The duodenum is visible posterior to the stomach as the dissection continues towards the short gastric vessels and the spleen.

The surgeon observes a visage bougie in place and notes the large left lobe of the liver, which complicates visualization. A small capsular tear on the liver is controlled with pressure. To retract the proximal part of the left lobe, a suture is used to elevate it like a canopy, while gauze is applied to distribute force and prevent further tearing. The suture is anchored with one end on the Nathanson retractor and the other through the falciform ligament.

Using the robotic platform, the surgeon manipulates and places sutures on the interior aspect of the abdomen. The diaphragm is visible as dissection progresses towards the left crura. The spleen is also visualized, indicating the correct direction of dissection. Some dissection occurs from the posterior aspect, with attention to the location of the left gastric vasculature.

The surgeon carefully dissects the filmy tissue along the lesser curvature of the stomach, ensuring the blood vessels remain intact. The cardiac region is fully mobilized, and the left crura is identified. The previously placed suture is removed, revealing the pulsating vessels along the lesser curvature.

The robotic arm retracts the liver using gauze to prevent injury to the liver capsule. The surgeon prepares for the first staple firing with a black load, ensuring not to impinge on the incisor. Both anterior and posterior aspects of the stomach are visualized for staple line imprecation. A large lipoma is identified at the gastroesophageal junction, and partial removal is planned to improve visualization of the angle of His.

The surgeon carefully avoids burning the underlying stomach while ensuring the redundant portion of the posterior fundus and cardia remains with the specimen. The specimen is moved aside, and the surgeon proceeds to imbricate the staple line using the Lambert technique.

The surgeon applies biological fibrin glue to the staple line to address oozing, following a negative methylene glue test. The staple line is reinforced without buttressing material, and there is no visible bleeding from the previous capsular tear.
